data.title: Micron: Don't Fall For The Low P/E - It's A Value Trap
data.description:
Introduction

Micron Technology (MU) is an interesting company. At the time of writing, it’s the only company in the S&P 500 with a “straight A” report card on Seeking Alpha’s Quant Factor ratings. It’s got a 4.99/5 Quant rating (ranked #2 out of all stocks covered by the system), an overall “Buy” rating from Seeking Alpha analysts, and a “Strong Buy” average rating from Wall Street analysts (28 rate it a strong buy, 10 buy, 5 hold, and no Wall Street analysts rate it a sell). What’s that adage about when something sounds too good to be true?

For a stock that’s surged nearly 300% in the last year, this has me asking, “What's the catch?” How can the stock appreciate this much and still look so cheap? The answer, in my view, is that Micron is the ultimate siren song stock. I see commentators and analysts mentioning metrics like P/E, EV/EBITDA, and PEG ratios, but the problem is that with this stock in particular, these are not buy signals but warning signs.

In this article, I lay out the rationale for Micron being a tempting but ultimately unwise investment. I then use a valuation model based on a projection of 2030 book value and apply a 1.5x price-to-book multiple on that value and then discount it to the present. Under optimistic assumptions, I get a fair value per share of $264. I don’t include a repricing catalyst or suggest trading the stock right now. But I argue that Micron’s behavior right now is right in line with its historical boom-bust pattern.

Background (Earnings Call)

Micron recently reported what is possibly the best quarter in the history of the semiconductor industry, and paradoxically the stock has since declined significantly. Revenues tripled, and gross margins hit 74.4%, and the company guided for 81% margins. On this news the stock entered a bear market within a matter of days, and as a result is currently one of the ‘cheapest’ stocks in the S&P 500 in terms of forward P/E. So how do we make sense of this apparent discrepancy?

Game Theory of an Oligopoly

There are three major manufacturers of DRAM and High Bandwidth Memory: Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix. Let’s say two of these companies decide to hold back production to keep memory prices high. This leaves the third with a massive incentive to expand production to capture market share. Because one player is likely to break rank in a three-player game, all three players are likely to break rank rather than collude.

Indulge me with an analogy to explain this dynamic. Let’s imagine that only three kids are allowed to sell lemonade in a wealthy neighborhood. In normal times they sell glasses of lemonade for $1 each. But all of a sudden, a massive heat wave hits exactly when construction crews come into the neighborhood to build a few new mansions. The thirsty, exhausted construction workers are willing to pay $5 per glass for the lemonade. The kids can’t make lemonade fast enough. To fill the demand, they need to stop squeezing lemonade with their bare hands and instead buy industrial juicers for $1000 a piece.

What happens in this dynamic? If only one of the kids (let’s call him Timmy) buys the juicer, he makes a killing, and the other kids lose out massively. Because no one wants to lose out, all three kids buy the industrial juicers. The heat wave and construction projects are expected to last a few more weeks, so the kids know there’s likely strong demand in the immediate future. But what happens in the long run? Eventually, all three kids have far too much capacity. To sell their gallons of lemonade before they spoil, the kids engage in a brutal race-to-the-bottom price war, undercutting each other until the cost of a glass of lemonade has crashed to a few cents a piece.

Now imagine a wealthy investor, Mr. Monty P. Moneybags, pulling up in his stretched limo on the hottest day of the year. He sees the line of construction workers circling the block to get a glass of lemonade from Timmy’s Lemonade Stand. Mr. Moneybags does some very flawed back-of-the-handkerchief math: he figures Timmy sells 1,000 glasses of lemonade per day for $5 each, with an 80% margin. That’s $4k per day, or about $1.5 million per year. Mr. Moneybags figures if he offers Timmy $1.5 million to buy the lemonade business, he’ll make his money back within a year. “I’m getting this great business for a 1.0x P/E multiple; what a steal!” Mr. Moneybags has fallen for the cyclical trap: he’s valued a cyclical commodity as though it’s a secular growth story.

This is what the market is worried about with Micron. If you’re an investor thinking about investing in Timmy’s Lemonade Stand, you would be a fool to judge the business based on a snapshot in the middle of a heat/construction wave. You can’t assume that the 1,000 glasses of lemonade sold every day for $5 each is sustainable in the long term. So right now, things look very strong. But the memory market is brutally cyclical. If, as has happened in the past, demand dries up, then the additional capacity built during the boom cycle guarantees a total erosion of margins during the bust cycle. Ironically, Micron’s forward P/E ratio is usually lowest when its stock price is highest. In this case, a low P/E is not a sign of a cheap stock; it’s a sign of a cyclical peak.

Why Is HBM So Expensive Today?

High Bandwidth Memory builds a skyscraper out of traditional DRAM. It’s essentially a new product: SK Hynix was the first to figure out the packaging process, while for quite a while Samsung failed. Micron is actually the smallest player out of the three. The process used to drill microscopic holes in DRAM that is required to turn DRAM into HBM is very difficult and energy intensive. Right now production yields are quite low, which leads to a lot of trashed product. That’s one reason HBM is so expensive at the moment: a low yield rate means there is a significant shortage.

The current argument for Micron’s stock is that HBM is a new type of product with more sustainable pricing power. Admittedly there are some important differences from past cycles—Micron strikes deals with chipmakers for long periods of guaranteed sales. So, at least for 2026, sales are locked-in in advance. In the lemonade analogy, Timmy has pre-sold the lemonade well in advance, so he doesn’t have to worry about collapsing demand in the short term. The construction workers are already on the hook for near-term purchases.

But ultimately it’s still made out of DRAM, and, in my view, the same economic dynamic likely applies to HBM as applies to DRAM. Ironically, increasing yields may actually hurt the market. Right now, chipmakers can’t get enough memory; Nvidia basically has to pay whatever prices Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix ask. But if yield rates increase, then supply immediately increases as well, without having to wait for new capacity to hit the market. Then customers’ pricing power increases overnight.

The irony is that in an oligopoly, what is good at the firm level tends to be self-defeating at the industry level. All three players are individually incentivized to push production yields higher, but if they all do, then they all lose pricing power. An oligopoly makes it difficult to keep yields low artificially since one player would disproportionately gain over the other two by increasing their own yields. If Micron alone could increase its yields while the other two were not able to increase yields, Micron would win temporarily. But I believe the most likely scenario is that each competitor pushes the others to greater efficiency. When that happens, supply increases and the memory market shifts from a seller’s market to a buyer’s market.

Moat Arbitrage

I would argue that in a scenario in which Micron wins, Nvidia (NVDA) wins more, and in a situation in which Nvidia loses, Micron loses more. Let’s examine what happens in each outcome:

AI Demand is Long-Lasting

Let’s say AI exceeds our wildest expectations and data center growth is absolutely insatiable for years to come. The problem for Micron is that it creates huge incentives for HBM capacity to expand to meet demand. When that happens, the massive margins that Micron currently enjoys will still likely vanish eventually. Nvidia, on the other hand, has a totally unique product backed up by a proprietary ecosystem that developers are locked into. Nvidia, therefore, has more control over how much it supplies to the market. It can optimize its CapEx to protect its margins—it doesn't have to surge capacity to a self-defeating extent because it doesn't have to worry as much about competitors with close substitutes. So in a world of surging demand, you'd still rather own the monopolist (or dominant firm, to be precise) than the oligopolist. Consequently, in the event of an AI-driven boom, NVDA > MU in my view.

AI Bubble Bursts

Now let’s think about the bearish scenario. Let’s say companies realize they bought too many GPUs and they decrease or pause orders. This definitely hurts Nvidia, but the company is still protected by its massive proprietary moat and CUDA ecosystem. Even in an upgrade/replacement cycle, I think Nvidia can retain a significant amount of pricing power. So far each new platform (Rubin, Blackwell, etc.) is a significant upgrade on the one before it. For Micron, the capacity being built today ensures memory oversupply in a cycle downturn. To service the fixed costs of this extra capacity, all three memory makers would be forced to slash prices. HBM is improving, but ultimately Micron is unlikely to be able to have a product that is differentiated much from what Samsung or SK Hynix has. In the bubble-bursting scenario, Micron likely loses all its pricing power very quickly. So in an AI bust, NVDA > MU still. If you're interested to see why I think this scenario may be the more likely of the two, check out my recent article discussing the development of AI.

Both companies right now look cheap on paper with low PE and PEG ratios relative to history. But Micron is far more exposed to the cutthroat nature of cyclical commodity economics. Yes, at the moment Micron looks very cheap, but this is the Mr. Moneybags fallacy.

History Repeating Itself

I don’t want to belabor the dot-com bubble analogy, but for Micron I think it is a relevant case study. In June 1998, Micron was trading around $11 per share. By July 2000 it was up to $97 per share. Sound familiar? By 2003, the stock had crashed to $7.50 per share. If you bought at the peak of frenzy in 2000, you would have had to hold the stock for 24 years to make your money back. In the late 1990s, there was insatiable memory demand as we built out the internet. Servers, routers, and home PCs all needed DRAM. But when the build-out slowed, memory prices crashed, taking MU’s stock price down with them. The chart below shows MU's price history from 1995 through 2023, before this latest upturn. You can see that in prior times when MU's stock gained value exponentially, it didn't tend to last.

In the dot-com bubble, compared to companies like Cisco, Micron’s valuation looked entirely reasonable based on metrics like the P/E multiple or EV/EBITDA multiple. But again, investors were looking at a snapshot in time and not factoring in the fundamental patterns of memory cycles. If you’re using a P/E or EV/EBITDA multiple to value Micron, you’re making the same error investors made in the late 90s. Those valuation multiples inherently assume earnings perpetuity. You’re committing the Mr. Moneybags fallacy. This mistake seems to be predictably repeated every time memory is in high demand. MU has seen peak-to-trough declines of 50% or more on six occasions since the 1990s.

Valuation

So what is Micron worth, and how should we value a company that exhibits such extreme cyclicality? From 2016 to 2018, operating income shot up from $234 million to $15 billion. It then dropped to $3 billion in 2020. Then it shot up to $10 billion, then dropped to -$3.5 billion. Now it’s at $28 billion in the most recent fiscal year reported. And forecasts are, of course, even better. But income and cash flow are almost guaranteed to be all over the map. That makes income- or cash flow-based valuation models difficult to use for this stock.

In my valuation model, I try to determine Micron’s book value in 2030 based on a clean surplus assumption. I use this methodology because it can allow us to model the impact of the current boom cycle on book value. Micron’s book value has been far more stable and predictable over time than its income or cash flows have:

To determine the change in book value over time, I assume 40% ROE in 2026, 50% in 2027, 30% in 2028, and 15% in 2029 and 2030 (note that Micron’s 5Y average ROE is 8%, so I’m assuming ROE remains well above historical averages). This grants the bullish assumption that memory demand will remain very high through about 2028 (when new capacity comes online). That gives an expected book value of $310 per share by 2030. Then I apply a 1.5x price-to-book multiple to get a fair value per share of $465 by the end of 2030. This I view as a generous assumption since it assumes that Micron will be able to extract economic profit in perpetuity (if we assumed zero economic profit after 2030, then fair value per share would just equal book value). So a 1.5x multiple of book value assumes that Micron will continue earning in excess of its cost of equity in perpetuity. In reality, there will likely be periods in which Micron has negative ROE followed by periods of booming demand with extremely high ROE.

Discounting that value back to the present using a required return on equity of 12% (derived from a CAPM with a beta of 1.5, a risk-free rate of 4.5%, and an equity risk premium of 5%) gives a fair value today of $264 per share. So the current share price is at about a 35% premium to my fair value based on this model.

Think about what I'm assuming happens: I'm assuming that Micron becomes one of the largest companies in the world by book value. It will have a greater book value than ExxonMobil and JP Morgan have today and slightly less than Saudi Aramco has today. And then Micron will consistently produce returns in excess of the cost of equity on that massive asset base. This is certainly not a bearish assumption. I'm granting an optimistic version of events over the next few years, and yet fair value per share based on those assumptions is still lower than the market price. That suggests that the stock really is priced for perfection.

Risks to My Thesis

The major risk to my thesis is that demand structurally outpaces supply for many more years than expected. In that case, Micron may be well undervalued right now. If data center spending continues its parabolic growth beyond 2029 or 2030, then the expanded capacity being built now for memory production may still not be sufficient to meet demand. But like I said before, I think that Nvidia is a safer bet if one believes that is the likeliest future. Nvidia likely wins if aggressive data center buildout continues for a long period of time. Micron needs both that and constrained memory capacity. Even if we continue rapidly building data centers, that only works for Micron if capacity doesn’t adapt to meet demand. It already is at the moment—all three players in the HBM market are spending billions to expand capacity.

Micron bulls will likely argue that I'm failing to appreciate the depth of the global memory demand shortage and that my valuation model should reflect that. The problem, though, is that such a shortage guarantees an arms race among the big three players. The incentive to be first to expand capacity enough to meet demand is so strong that it makes a 5-10 year memory shortage very unlikely, even if AI infrastructure spending continues to grow year over year.

Conclusion

I’m well aware that this thesis may look downright foolish in the near term, especially if the overall market recovers from the current dip anytime soon. It would not surprise me if Micron’s share price doubles again in the short term. I’m not attaching any potential repricing catalyst to this analysis either. But I am warning investors that you may be buying into a classic trap here. As long as you don’t spring the trap, you might be in the clear. But I believe that eventually the trap will snap shut and punish investors who hold onto the stock for too long, as it has done before.

As Sir John Templeton said, “The four most dangerous words in investing are: ‘This time it’s different.’” In 1995 people thought memory demand would be permanent because everyone was buying a PC. The stock crashed 80%. In 2000, the story was server demand: the stock crashed 93%. In 2018 the cloud boom was going to make memory a permanent institutional product, no longer tied to consumer PC upgrades. The stock crashed nearly 60% then. Now it’s that HBM is a new paradigm, and so memory demand will now really be permanent. Though it’s hard to say when the peak will occur, I believe a similar cycle is likely to play out again.

data.title: Bonds Won't Save You From The Next Recession
data.description:
I started studying economics and finance in 2019, and one of the first things that I learned was that equities tend to have a negative correlation with bonds. Therefore, if you add bonds to your equity portfolio, you are essentially hedging against the downside risk. In theory, if a recession occurs, equities drop, but part of the losses are going to be offset by bond price appreciation. So, if a 100% equity portfolio drops 40% during a severe recession, a 60/40 (60% equities, 40% bonds) portfolio might decline by just 15-20%.

For years, financial advisors promoted the 60/40 portfolio as a way to benefit from bullish periods while reducing your downside risk, but I believe that this strategy no longer works. Why? Because the correlation between bonds and equity is no longer negative. In my opinion, bonds won’t protect you against the next recession.

Where Does This Bias Come From?

To understand why the 60/40 portfolio no longer works to protect you against the downside risk, I believe it is important to first point out why it got so popular.

People love the idea of remaining invested in the stock market while taking less risk thanks to the bond’s exposure. The 60/40 portfolio was the ideal compromise for people with a low-medium risk tolerance, and the performance achieved across the “lost decade” further fueled their conviction.

From 2001 to 2013, the stock market (red line) went nowhere because the US economy faced two significant recessions. But the 60/40 portfolio (blue line) was up about 30% during the same period. Not only that, in fact the downsides were much less pronounced, something that is very important to help you not panic.

After such a great performance during one of the worst periods ever to be invested, it is not a coincidence that the 60/40 portfolio started to gain weight in the financial community. In 2011, Vanguard launched LifeStrategy 60/40, basically an ETF that replicates the 60/40 approach, and had a pretty decent success. You don’t even have to rebalance each weight on your own.

The 60/40 portfolio performed pretty well also during the Covid crash and protected investors from higher losses. So, if this portfolio worked during the last 3 recessions, why am I saying that it won’t work in the future?

A Hypothesis That Is Far From Obvious

The biggest problem with this strategy is that it takes for granted that the macroeconomic context won’t change. And just to be clear, for macroeconomic context, I am not referring to small changes in the interest rates but a structural shift in the economy that takes decades to get back to the previous levels.

Finance courses teach you that bonds will protect you against a stock market decline, but just because this was the case from 2000 to 2021. The entire financial industry is set on this assumption because it has forgotten how things were in the previous century. Take a look at this chart; I find it enlightening.

As you can see, over the last 100 years, the correlation between stocks and bonds was actually positive most of the time. In other words, a negative correlation is not the norm, or at least it is not as obvious as we might think. At this point a question arises: what’s the most important factor that affects the equity/bond correlation? The inflation rate, and the following chart proves it.

On the Y axis we have the 5-year equity/bond correlation, and on the X axis we have the 5-year annual inflation rate. As you can see, the results are quite evident:

  • During 1970-2000 (green dots), the 5-year annual inflation rate was never below 2%, and it was above 5% most of the time. During that market cycle that lasted 3 decades, the correlation between equity/bonds has always been positive (green dots always above 0). Simply put, bonds wouldn’t have helped you to face the downside risk.
  • During 1929-1970 (blue dots), we have had mixed results. The Great Depression (1929) caused a severe deflation, which was favorable for bonds, but after WW2, the US economy inflated the debt away. That was definitely negative for bonds performance. Overall, even though this period of four decades suffered a major deflation, most of the time the equity-bonds correlation was positive. High inflation was much more frequent.
  • During 2000-2021 (yellow dots), the game changed. Most of the time the equity-bond correlation was negative, so buying bonds was a smart way to diversify your portfolio. During this market phase, the 5-year annual inflation has almost always been below 3%.

In light of these considerations, we can make a fundamental observation: bonds protect your equity portfolio as long as the inflation rate stays below 3%.

Over the years investors took for granted that the inflation rate would have always fluctuated between 1% and 3%, but history is actually telling us that this is not the norm. Let me show you the previous chart once again.

As you can see, the distribution of the yellow dots (2000-2021) is much more concentrated compared to 1926-1970 and 1970-2000. So, if we really want to draw some conclusions here, I would argue that the anomaly is what we have experienced over the last two decades.

Understanding in which long-term macroeconomic cycle we are makes all the difference, and I believe that in 2022 we entered a new phase similar to 1970-2000 (2022 was the worst year for a 60/40 portfolio since 1937).

As you can see, TLT (an ETF composed of US long-term bonds) didn’t offset the 2022 stock market decline, and that was kind of surprising. Weren’t bonds supposed to protect us when hard times come? At first, I believed that 2022 was an anomaly, but as time went by, my opinion changed. We had other stock market declines over the following 4 years, and TLT has been helpful just during Liberation Day. Now, the stock market is in correction territory, and TLT is once again following the same direction.

In other words, a structural change in the macroeconomic context doesn’t happen overnight but over the years. The year 2022 was the turning point, but over the last years we had several indications that support my thesis. Here is another dot graph that helps you understand the evolution of this new phase.

As you can see, the period 2000-2021 (blue dots) showed a negative correlation between bonds and equities most of the time, and we already knew that. But the period October 2021-February 2023 (pink dots) presented a major shift: dots were heading upward. With the March 2023-January 2025 (purple dots) period, I believe we had the confirmation: dots were stably in the upper area of the chart, where the correlation is significantly positive. This is the gradual shift I was talking about.

Final Thoughts

People believe that diversification is about how many assets you own, but the most important factor is how correlated they are to each other. If you buy 100 assets that are positively correlated, you are not really diversifying. And in this phase, you are not really diversifying your equity portfolio if you buy TLT.

At this point the $1 million question is, when will TLT become a valid hedge against recessions once again? This is not something that I can predict with certainty, but I am really confident that it won’t happen anytime soon. As I previously showed you, this kind of cycle lasts decades, and we have just entered the fourth year.

In addition, the Middle East crisis is intensifying, and there is nothing that makes me think that inflation won’t go significantly up over the next months. As I have stated from day 1, this war shouldn’t be underestimated, and it is consistent with the new macroeconomic context in which bonds can’t protect you against falling equities.

We are likely going through a period where inflation will systematically be higher than 2%, and we might even face negative real yields at some point. In fact, inflating the debt away is one of the few options governments have to make their huge debt more sustainable. (This is something that I discussed in depth in a recent article).

During inflationary periods, gold tends to be a better hedge compared to bonds, especially if central banks can’t increase interest rates at the same pace as inflation. I believe this is the case, as most developed economies present a huge debt compared to GDP, and dealing with higher interest payments would be the final blow.
